perturbation.original_text: Failure by Premier to meet payment terms of any invoice shall result in interest being imposed on any unpaid balance at the rate of [***] per month, pro rata on a daily basis for partial months, accrued from its due date or in the event such rate exceeds that permitted to be charged by law, the maximum rate permitted by law.
perturbation.changed_text: Failure by Premier to meet payment terms of any invoice shall result in interest being imposed on any unpaid balance at the rate of 25% per month, pro rata on a daily basis for partial months, accrued from its due date or in the event such rate exceeds that permitted to be charged by law, the maximum rate permitted by law.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the interest rate to 25% per month (300% annually) introduces a potential violation of usury laws. California, where Premier Nutrition Corporation has a principal place of business, has limits on the maximum interest rate that can be charged.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Constitution sets the usury limit at 10% per annum or 5% over the discount rate established by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco. Imposing a 25% monthly interest rate (300% annually) clearly exceeds this limit, unless a specific exemption applies, rendering the provision potentially unenforceable under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Codes Display Text skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Group Code Section Group Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ Add To My Favorites Code Text * California Constitution - CONS ARTICLE XV USURY [SECTION 1- SECTION 1.] ( Article 15 added June 8, 1976, by Prop. 14, clause 35. Res.Ch. 5, 1976. ) SECTION 1. The rate of interest upon the loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, or on accounts after demand, shall be 7 percent per annum but it shall be competent for the parties to any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action to contract in writing for a rate of interest: (1) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, if the money, goods, or things in action are for use primarily for personal, family, or household purposes, at a rate not exceeding 10 percent per annum; provided, however, that any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action the proceeds of which are used primarily for the purchase, construction or improvement of real property shall not be deemed to be a use primarily for personal, family or household purposes; or (2) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action for any use other than specified in paragraph (1), at a rate not exceeding the higher of (a) 10 percent per annum or (b) 5 percent per annum plus the rate prevailing on the 25th day of the month preceding the earlier of (i) the date of execution of the contract to make the loan or forbearance, or (ii) the date of making the loan or forbearance established by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on advances to member banks under Sections 13 and 13a of the Federal Reserve Act as now in effect or hereafter from time to time amended (or if there is no such single determinable rate of advances, the closest counterpart of such rate as shall be designated by the Superintendent of Banks of the State of California unless some other person or agency is delegated such authority by the Legislature). No person, association, copartnership or corporation shall by charging any fee, bonus, commission, discount or other compensation receive from a borrower more than the interest authorized by this section upo
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a 25% monthly interest rate (300% annually), which contradicts California's usury laws. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that the rate of interest shall be 7 percent per annum, and allows for a rate not exceeding 10 percent per annum for loans primarily for personal, family, or household purposes. For other uses, it allows a rate not exceeding either 10 percent per annum or 5 percent per annum plus the rate established by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco. The 25% monthly rate significantly exceeds these limits, creating a legal contradiction and potential unenforceability under California law.

perturbation.original_text: Heritage agrees to provide to Premier each FDA Form 483 and any related Establishment Inspection Report ("EIR") that is received from the FDA by Heritage or Jasper, along with any response provided to the regulatory authority by Heritage or Jasper, as long as this Agreement is in effect. Heritage agrees to do so within [***] of Heritage or Jasper receiving the Report or of sending the response as appropriate.
perturbation.changed_text: Heritage agrees to provide to Premier each FDA Form 483 and any related Establishment Inspection Report ("EIR") that is received from the FDA by Heritage or Jasper, along with any response provided to the regulatory authority by Heritage or Jasper, as long as this Agreement is in effect. Heritage agrees to do so within 90 days of Heritage or Jasper receiving the Report or of sending the response as appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the provision of FDA 483 and EIR reports to 90 days may conflict with the FDA's expectation of prompt corrective action and communication, especially if the findings relate to serious violations of Current Good Manufacturing Practice (CGMP) regulations. The FDA expects timely responses, and delaying notification could impede Premier's ability to address potential issues quickly.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 111.75 requires that you take appropriate actions to ensure that you implement preventive controls and corrective actions that are adequate to significantly minimize or prevent significant hazards, as well as document the implementation of these controls and actions. While this regulation doesn't explicitly dictate the timeframe, a 90-day delay for notifying the other party is not in accordance with 'appropriate action' within a reasonable time and could be considered a failure to comply with CGMP requirements, if those hazards require urgent correction.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a potential contradiction by setting a fixed 90-day deadline for providing FDA reports, which conflicts with the legal expectation of 'appropriate action' to minimize or prevent significant hazards as indicated in 21 CFR § 111.75. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that a 90-day delay may not align with 'appropriate action' and could be considered a failure to comply with CGMP requirements if urgent correction is needed. While neither scraped snippet contains the full text of 21 CFR § 111.75, the Law Explanation makes it clear that the FDA requires timely responses. The delay introduced undermines the promptness expected for corrective action, potentially violating CGMP requirements.

perturbation.original_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement immediately without notice should the other Party fail to cure, within [***] after receipt of written notice thereof, any material breach of its obligations or duties hereunder, provided, however that in the event of a material breach that cannot be cured within [***], a Party shall not be deemed in default if it commences curing such default within the [***] period, notifies the other Party of that commencement by e-mail, and thereafter cures such default within [***] of the original written notice thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement immediately without notice should the other Party fail to cure, within 5 days after receipt of written notice thereof, any material breach of its obligations or duties hereunder, provided, however that in the event of a material breach that cannot be cured within 5 days, a Party shall not be deemed in default if it commences curing such default within the 5 day period, notifies the other Party of that commencement by e-mail, and thereafter cures such default within 10 days of the original written notice thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Providing only 5 days to cure a material breach might be considered unreasonable, especially for complex manufacturing issues. California law requires contracts to be fair and reasonable; a court may find a 5-day cure period insufficient, thus making the termination clause potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 2-302 addresses unconscionable contracts or clauses. If a court finds that a clause was unconscionable at the time it was made, the court may refuse to enforce the contract, enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or limit the application of the unconscionable clause to avoid an unconscionable result. A 5-day cure period may be deemed unconscionable if it does not provide a reasonable opportunity to cure a material breach, particularly for complex manufacturing or supply chain issues.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2302. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text
perturbation.justification: The changed text shortens the cure period for a material breach to 5 days, and the Perturbation Explanation suggests this may be unreasonable under California law, especially for complex manufacturing issues. The Law Explanation further references California Commercial Code § 2-302, addressing unconscionable contracts. The shortened cure period may be deemed unconscionable if it doesn't provide reasonable opportunity to cure a material breach, potentially contradicting the need for fairness in contracts. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms the existence of California Code, COM 2302, making it applicable here.
